content: The features of Indus cities, such as a grid-plan, a carefully engineered civic layout, a uniform and distinctive scheme of small oblong dwellings, brick-paved floors, and furnaces with intense vitrification of the brick lining, suggest a well-organized society. The presence of a citadel and a lower city, with a possible gap between them that may have been occupied by a canal or a branch of the river, also indicates government planning.

The Indus Civilization conducted trade with other regions, including Sumeria, through the Persian Gulf. This trade seems to have culminated under Sargon of Agade around 2350 B.C. and was revived under King Ur-Nammu. The Indus Valley was also connected to other regions through ancient trade routes, such as the one that lies in the direction of Kandahar.

The features of Indus cities that demonstrate advanced urban planning include a grid-plan, with the citadel being a roughly parallelogram shape, approximately 460 yards from north to south and 215 yards from east to west. The city also appears to have had a carefully engineered civic layout, with a possible canal or branch of the river between the citadel and the main body of the town. Additionally, the city had a well-planned system of streets, lanes, and dwellings, with each house having a uniform design and being entered through an oblique passage designed to secure privacy. The presence of a compound wall and brick-paved floors also suggests a high level of urban planning.

The features of Indus cities, such as a grid-plan, carefully engineered civic layout, well-planned system of streets, lanes, and dwellings, and the presence of a compound wall and brick-paved floors, suggest a high level of urban planning and a well-organized society.
